attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the labour ward with her partner. She is 39 weeks pregnant with her first child and reports regular, painful contractions every five minutes for the past two hours. She has no significant medical history and takes only prenatal vitamins.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable but coping well with the contractions. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. On abdominal examination, the uterus is term size with regular contractions palpable. Vaginal examination reveals the cervix is 3 cm dilated and 80% effaced, with the fetal head at station -2.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the current stage of labour for this patient?
options: Active phase of first stage of labour
Latent phase of first stage of labour
attributes.explanation:
The first stage of labour is characterised by regular uterine contractions leading to progressive cervical dilation. It is divided into two phases: latent and active.

The latent phase of first stage of labour typically involves cervical dilation from 0 to 4 cm, accompanied by regular contractions. This patient's cervix is 3 cm dilated, which is consistent with the end of the latent phase. The transition to active labour typically occurs around 4-6 cm dilation.

The active phase of first stage of labour is characterised by more rapid cervical dilation, typically from 4-6 cm to 10 cm. This patient has not yet entered this phase, as her cervix is only 3 cm dilated.

The second stage of labour begins when the cervix is fully dilated (10 cm) and ends with the birth of the baby. This patient's cervix is only 3 cm dilated, so she has not reached this stage.

The third stage of labour involves the delivery of the placenta and membranes. This occurs after the baby is born, which has not happened yet in this scenario.

Prodromal labour, also known as 'false labour', refers to contractions that may be regular for a period of time but do not lead to cervical dilation or effacement. This patient has clear cervical changes, indicating she is in true labour, not prodromal labour.

It's important to note that labour is a continuous process, and the transitions between stages can be gradual. Proper assessment of labour progress involves not just cervical dilation, but also the frequency and intensity of contractions, cervical effacement, and fetal station. Regular monitoring is crucial to ensure the well-being of both mother and baby throughout the labour process.
learningPoints: The first stage of labour is characterised by regular uterine contractions and cervical dilation up to 10 cm.
The second stage of labour begins with full cervical dilation and ends with the birth of the baby.
The third stage of labour involves the delivery of the placenta and membranes.
references: Hutchison J, Mahdy H, Hutchison J. Stages of Labor. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 [updated 2023 Jan 30]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK544290/
